The proposed work introduces multi-conditional guidance for diffusion models, successfully generating polymers that satisfy multi-property constraints, achieving a higher rank than 30 in any set of the single-conditional generated graphs.
The idea of multi-conditional guidance is implemented for diffusion models, leading to the successful generation of polymers satisfying multi-property constraints.
Graphs are generated aiming to intersect with polymers meeting multi-property constraints.

QV E and QEV are matrices defined based on the co-occurrence frequencies of atom and bond types in training molecular graphs. They represent the probability distributions of co-occurring atom types for each bond type.
mV E is the transpose of mEV and represents the probability of co-occurring atom types for each bond type.
QEV and QV E are components combined to form XtG. They are defined as linear combinations with respect to probabilities of co-occurring atom types.

LSTM-HC surpasses many baselines, achieving lower average MAE on polymer properties and higher rankings on small molecular properties.

MARS effectively manages synthetic scores for polymers but exhibits a larger MAE in gas permeability conditions compared to other baselines. GDSS performs well in gas permeability control but underperforms Graph GA and MARS in terms of the synthetic score condition.
DiGress v2 and MOOD, although equipped with the predictor guidance, still exhibit limited condition control compared to their unconditional counterparts over polymer and small molecule tasks.

Graph DiT significantly improves diffusion models and achieves the best multi-conditional performance in all tasks. In polymer tasks, Graph DiT reduces MAE on all gas permeability conditions, averaging +17.8% improvement over the best baseline LSTM-HC. For small molecule tasks, Graph DiT consistently ranks top-1 in condition controllability.

LSTM-HC consistently ranks top-1 in condition controllability with over 0.9 accuracy in categorical conditions. Graph DiT shows better controllability performance due to minimal rule-based post-generation processing.
